content.text: What It Is

An Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) for Generative AI defines the boundaries within which employees, contractors, and integrated systems may use AI-powered tools. The scope section establishes who and what the policy covers, while the prohibited-uses section enumerates categories of activity the organization explicitly forbids regardless of the tool used or the intent of the user.

Unlike generic IT acceptable-use policies, a GenAI AUP must address the unique risks introduced by large language models and multimodal systems: hallucinated outputs presented as facts, synthesis of personal data at scale, generation of synthetic media, and the ease with which these tools lower the barrier to producing harmful or fraudulent content.
A well-scoped AUP distinguishes between
absolutely prohibited
uses such as generating malware or CSAM,
conditionally permitted
uses that require pre-approval such as processing PHI through an approved LLM, and
generally permitted
uses that fall within everyday productivity.
Why It Matters

Organizations deploying GenAI tools face regulatory, legal, and reputational exposure when employees misuse them. A clearly articulated AUP with explicit prohibited-use categories creates the legal and contractual baseline for enforcement, demonstrates due diligence to regulators, and protects the organization when violations occur.

Without a defined AUP, ambiguity about what is allowed becomes a compliance gap. Courts and regulators increasingly examine whether organizations had adequate policies in place before a harm occurred. The AUP is the first layer of that governance defense.
The AUP does not replace technical controls. It creates the normative framework that technical controls enforce and that HR and Legal reference when escalating violations.
Benefits
Establishes clear expectations, reducing accidental misuse by well-intentioned employees
Creates enforceable obligations that support disciplinary action and, if needed, legal claims
Demonstrates regulatory due diligence under GDPR, CCPA, EU AI Act, and sector-specific rules
Reduces liability when third-party contractors misuse AI tools on company systems
Provides a reference point for security teams configuring content filters and audit rules
Enables formal exception workflows that keep the business moving while maintaining oversight
Trade-offs
Breadth vs. precision:
A broad policy covers unforeseen cases but can block legitimate uses; a narrow policy is precise but quickly becomes outdated as AI capabilities evolve
Centralization vs. flexibility:
Centralizing approval for edge cases adds safety but slows teams; decentralizing to business units risks inconsistency
Explicitness vs. maintainability:
Exhaustive prohibited-use lists require frequent updates as new model capabilities emerge
Employee productivity:
Overly restrictive AUPs can drive shadow AI use, which is harder to monitor than sanctioned tools
How It Works
A GenAI AUP is structured around three axes:
who
it covers (scope of persons and systems),
what
it covers (scope of tools and data), and
what is forbidden
(the prohibited-use taxonomy).
Scope of Persons

The policy applies to all individuals who access company-sanctioned or company-owned systems where GenAI tools are available. This includes full-time employees, part-time staff, contractors, consultants, and third-party system integrators whose code runs in company environments.
Scope of Systems and Tools

The policy covers any LLM or GenAI service accessed via company credentials, company-issued devices, company networks, or company-managed API keys whether that is a commercial SaaS product such as Microsoft 365 Copilot or GitHub Copilot, an internally hosted model, or a third-party API integration.
Prohibited-Use Taxonomy

Prohibited uses are organized into categories based on the nature of the harm they prevent. Categories typically include synthetic media and impersonation, personal data and privacy violations, academic and intellectual property fraud, discriminatory outputs, security and malware generation, regulated-domain violations, and market manipulation.
Architecture

attrs.alt: AUP Scope and Prohibited Uses Coverage Map

content.text: The diagram shows three pillars of an AUP: the scope defining who and what is covered, the prohibited-use categories defining forbidden actions, and the enforcement layer defining how violations are detected and addressed. Technical controls implement and reinforce the policy but do not replace it.
When to Use It
At initial GenAI tool deployment before employees gain access to any AI-assisted product
When onboarding contractors or vendors who will interact with AI-enabled systems
When adding a new AI capability such as moving from text-only to multimodal that introduces new risk categories
As part of annual policy refresh cycles or whenever a significant model or product change occurs
When a compliance audit, customer due-diligence request, or regulatory inquiry requires evidence of policy governance
How to Implement
Implementing a GenAI AUP requires collaboration between Legal, Security, HR, and the teams deploying AI tools. The following phases structure a typical implementation.
Phase 1: Inventory and Risk Assessment

Catalog all GenAI tools in use or planned. For each, identify the data types that flow through it, the user populations, and the harm scenarios specific to that tool. This inventory drives the scope and prohibited-use definitions.
Phase 2: Draft Policy

Draft the AUP using the taxonomy below as a baseline. Have Legal review for jurisdictional compliance and HR review for alignment with employment contracts. Distinguish absolutely prohibited uses from conditionally permitted uses that require approval.
Phase 3: Approval and Version Control

Route the draft through your governance committee including CISO, DPO, and General Counsel. Tag the approved version with a version number and effective date. Store it in your policy management system with audit history.
Phase 4: Communication and Training
Publish the policy on the intranet, send an all-staff communication, and integrate AUP training into onboarding and annual compliance training. Require acknowledgment or attestation.
Phase 5: Review Cycle
Schedule annual reviews and trigger out-of-cycle reviews when a new high-risk AI capability is deployed, a significant violation occurs, or a relevant regulation changes.
Examples
The following policy template covers the prohibited-uses section. Adapt it to your organization's regulatory context.

## 4. Prohibited Uses of Generative AI Tools

The following uses are strictly prohibited. This list is non-exhaustive.

### 4.1 Synthetic Media and Impersonation
- Generating deepfake images, audio, or video of any real person
- Creating synthetic media that impersonates executives or public figures
- Producing content designed to deceive recipients about its AI-generated
  nature in contexts where disclosure is required

### 4.2 Personal Data and Privacy
- Processing special-category personal data (health, biometric, financial)
  through any AI tool not explicitly approved for that data type
- Prompting AI tools with PII to generate profiles or decisions about
  identifiable individuals without a lawful basis
- Using AI to aggregate or de-anonymize data sets beyond permitted scope

### 4.3 Academic and Intellectual Property Fraud
- Submitting AI-generated content as original work where prohibited
- Reproducing copyrighted text or code at a scale exceeding fair use
- Generating content that knowingly infringes third-party IP

### 4.4 Discriminatory and Harmful Outputs
- Generating content that discriminates based on protected characteristics
- Creating content that harasses, defames, or threatens individuals
- Generating instructions for illegal activity or causing physical harm

### 4.5 Security and Malware
- Using AI to generate malware, exploits, or phishing content
- Enumerating vulnerabilities in systems the user is not authorized to test
- Automating credential attacks or unauthorized access attempts

### 4.6 Regulated Domains Without Approval
- Generating medical diagnoses, legal advice, or financial recommendations
  for external distribution without sign-off from the relevant professional team
- Making automated decisions about individuals in high-risk domains
  without a completed DPIA

### 4.7 Market and Financial Manipulation
- Generating false statements to influence securities prices
- Automating communications to manipulate supplier or customer pricing
Real-World Example

A global financial services firm rolled out GitHub Copilot and a commercial LLM chat tool to 4,000 developers and analysts. Before launch, Legal and Security co-authored an AUP with three prohibited-use categories specific to their sector: prompting with customer transaction data outside the approved DLP perimeter, generating regulatory filings for external distribution without human review, and using AI to generate trading signals without passing model-risk-management review.

When an analyst used the LLM to draft a compliance memo that was sent externally without human review, the AUP provided the basis for a formal disciplinary process and remediation. The incident triggered an update adding a fourth category: AI-generated content for external communication requires a secondary human review gate.
Common Mistakes
Scope limited to employees only:
Contractors and API integrations are often higher-risk vectors for misuse and must be covered explicitly
Prohibited list tied to specific model names:
Model names change frequently; policy should target capabilities and data types, not product names
No exception process:
Without a formal exception workflow, employees work around policy rather than through it
No distinction between absolute and conditional prohibitions:
Treating everything as a hard prohibition makes the policy unworkable for legitimate edge cases
Policy not version-controlled:
Without a version number and effective date, enforcement becomes legally fragile
Excluded from third-party contracts:
AUP obligations must flow down to vendors through contractual clauses
Best Practices
Draft in plain language so employees without legal training understand their obligations
Organize prohibited uses by harm type rather than by tool so the policy stays valid as the tool landscape changes
Pair each prohibited category with a short rationale explaining the harm it prevents
Define a named exception process with an approver and a documented approval record
Version the policy and publish a changelog so employees know what changed and when
Cross-reference the AUP in employment contracts, vendor agreements, and system-access terms
Test the policy annually by walking through recent AI-use incidents to verify they would be caught
Performance & Scalability

An AUP is a governance artifact rather than a technical system, but its operational performance matters. Key scalability concerns include keeping the policy current as AI capabilities expand faster than annual review cycles, operationalizing the exception process at scale without creating a bottleneck, and ensuring the policy is consistently applied across geographically distributed teams subject to different local regulations.

Larger organizations typically create a tiered AUP: a global baseline that applies everywhere, supplemented by regional annexes that address jurisdiction-specific requirements such as EU AI Act obligations for high-risk use cases or China generative AI regulation requirements.
Tooling & Ecosystem
Policy management platforms:
ServiceNow Policy and Compliance, Hyperproof, Drata, and Vanta provide version control, attestation tracking, and audit trails for policy documents
DLP integration:
Microsoft Purview, Nightfall, and Symantec DLP can enforce data-type restrictions by blocking or flagging prompts containing PII or regulated data before they reach an LLM
Content filtering:
Enterprise LLM APIs including Azure OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, and AWS Bedrock include configurable content filters that technically enforce categories like harmful content generation
AI governance platforms:
Credo AI, Holistic AI, and Arthur AI provide policy-to-control mapping, helping teams trace which technical controls implement which AUP prohibitions

Security & Reliability

The AUP is a governance control that sits above technical controls. Its security contribution is establishing accountability: when a prohibited action occurs, the AUP determines whether it was a policy violation subject to disciplinary action or a technical failure subject to control improvement. Without the AUP, all incidents are classified as technical failures, removing personal accountability.

Reliability concerns include ensuring the policy is accessible to employees when needed, ensuring enforcement is consistent because selective enforcement undermines both policy credibility and legal defensibility, and ensuring the exception process does not become a rubber stamp by tracking exception rates and auditing approved exceptions annually.
Exception Process Design

Every AUP needs a functioning exception process or employees will either bypass the policy entirely or halt legitimate work. A well-designed exception process requires the requester to describe the business need, identify the specific prohibition being waived, propose compensating controls, and name the relevant data types involved. The approver, typically the CISO or DPO, then makes a documented decision with a time-limited approval and a review date.
Define the exception request form with mandatory fields: business justification, scope of data, duration, and compensating controls
Set a maximum approval duration, commonly 90 days, with mandatory renewal requiring fresh justification
Log all approved exceptions in a central register and include them in annual policy reviews
Track the ratio of exception requests to approvals; a high approval rate suggests the underlying prohibition is too broad
Notify the requestor's manager and relevant data steward when an exception is approved so oversight is not siloed
Policy Scope for Agentic AI

Standard AUPs written for LLM chat tools may not adequately cover agentic AI systems that take autonomous actions. When an agent browses the web, executes code, sends emails, or creates files without direct human oversight for each step, the prohibited-use categories need explicit extension. Agentic scope additions typically include: prohibition on agents that can exfiltrate data to external destinations without audit trail, prohibition on agents that acquire resources or permissions beyond what is needed for the stated task, and requirements that all agent actions be logged and replayable for forensic review.
Key Takeaways
The AUP scope must explicitly cover employees, contractors, third-party vendors, and API integrations
Prohibited uses should be organized by harm type rather than by tool name to remain valid as the landscape changes
Distinguish absolutely prohibited uses from conditionally permitted uses that require pre-approval
An exception process with a named approver and documented record is essential to avoid policy circumvention
Version-control the policy with an effective date and publish a changelog on every update
AUP obligations must flow down to vendors and contractors through contractual clauses
Technical controls implement the policy but do not replace it; accountability requires documented human obligations
Schedule annual reviews and trigger out-of-cycle reviews when new AI capabilities deploy or significant violations occur
